title: Gemini model 'gemini-3-flash-preview' likely invalid for the legacy @google/generative-ai SDK — live AI silently dead at launch
detail: Line 135: `model: 'gemini-3-flash-preview'`. package.json pins `@google/generative-ai@^0.24.1` — the LEGACY Google Generative AI SDK (superseded by `@google/genai`). That SDK targets the generativelanguage v1beta endpoint whose served catalog is the Gemini 1.5/2.0 family; Gemini-3 models route through the newer @google/genai unified SDK. If the ID is not served, every `model.generateContent()` (geminiClient.ts:296) throws, `recordFailure()` (:317) increments, and after CIRCUIT_BREAKER_THRESHOLD=3 the breaker opens — the headline client-side Gemini feature falls back to heuristic-only with NO user-visible error (connectionError is never set at runtime). The GREEN baseline does not catch this: all 1199 tests mock the SDK; no test asserts/contract-checks the model string (grep confirmed). Corroborating same-root evidence the running model isn't what the code assumes: comments/logs are inconsistent — geminiClient.ts:146 logs 'Initialized with Gemini 2.0 Flash', aiEngine.ts:3866 says 'Gemini Flash 3'; and pricing constants aiConfigStore.ts:26-27 ($0.075 in / $0.30 out, 'as of Dec 2024') are 1.5-Flash numbers, so the cost overlay is wrong for any other model. Only a live smoke call against the user's key resolves validity definitively.
fix: Owner decision: confirm the exact model ID the user's API key + @google/generative-ai 0.24.1 actually serves (live smoke test). If Gemini 3 is intended, migrate to @google/genai and set a verified ID; otherwise set a known-served ID (e.g. a current 2.0/2.5-Flash). In the SAME change, fix the stale '2.0 Flash'/'Flash 3' log+comment strings and re-check the GEMINI_FLASH_*_COST constants against the chosen model's published pricing.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: Host trusts unvalidated, spoofable fields from untrusted guest messages (no payload validation, no default case)
detail: handleMessage (:280) switches on message.type with zero shape/identity validation; incoming data arrives as `data as MultiplayerMessage` (PeerConnection.ts:50) — an unchecked cast. Concrete authority/spoofing harms, not a crash (PeerConnection.ts:47-55 wraps handling in a silent try/catch, so malformed payloads are dropped, not thrown): (1) PLAYER_UPDATE → `store.updateRemotePlayer(message.payload.id, message.payload)` (:285) lets ANY guest overwrite ANOTHER player's position/state by spoofing payload.id. (2) CHAT (:355) takes guest-supplied `from`/`fromName` verbatim via addChatMessage — trivial impersonation. (3) PLAYER_JOIN/PLAYER_LEAVE (:288,:302) are accepted from guests, letting a guest inject or evict phantom players. (4) No `default` branch — unknown types silently ignored, and any non-string/NaN numeric field flows straight into the store and the 3D render path unguarded. Peers are explicitly untrusted (public PeerJS cloud server, predictable host ID `millos-{roomCode}`).
fix: Add a validateMessage(peerId, message) guard at the top of handleMessage: assert message.type is a known literal, assert payload exists and required fields are correct primitive types (finite numbers for positions/rotation, string ids), and reject any message whose payload.id/from does not match the authenticated sender peerId for PLAYER_UPDATE/CHAT (host should derive identity from the connection, not the payload). Add a `default:` that logs+drops. Keep it in MultiplayerManager; payload type literals already exist in multiplayer/types.ts.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: Silent swallow of incoming data with a misleading 'parse' comment; no message-shape guard at the wrapper boundary
detail: Lines 47-55: `this.connection.on('data', (data) => { try { const message = data as MultiplayerMessage; this.handleMessage(message); } catch { /* Failed to parse message - ignore */ } })`. The comment is wrong — PeerJS already deserialized the object, so nothing is being parsed; the try/catch silently swallows ANY error thrown downstream in handleMessage AND the entire MultiplayerManager.handleMessage chain (invoked via the onMessage callback at :94). That means real bugs/exceptions in message handling vanish with no log. The cast `data as MultiplayerMessage` also provides no runtime safety. This is the boundary where a `typeof message?.type === 'string'` guard belongs.
fix: Replace the bare cast with a minimal runtime guard: if (!message || typeof (message as { type?: unknown }).type !== 'string') return; then handleMessage. In the catch, log via logger.multiplayer.warn with the error instead of swallowing, and correct the comment to 'handler error' not 'parse'.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: Runtime Gemini failures (429/quota/network) never surface to the user — connectionError set only at setup time
detail: connectionError is written only during key validation (aiConfigStore.ts:487, :497) and cleared on success (:509). There is NO distinct handling for 429 / RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED / quota anywhere (grep: zero hits for 429/quota/RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED across src). Once connected, every runtime failure path — geminiClient.generateContent catch (geminiClient.ts:318) and generateStrategicDecision catch (aiEngine.ts:3951) — only calls logger.* and returns null; the circuit breaker may open after 3 failures (geminiClient.ts:201) with no UI signal. Net effect: when the user's free-tier key hits a rate/quota limit, the strategic AI silently stops producing decisions and the UI keeps showing 'connected' with no explanation. isConnected() at :158 also reports false once the breaker trips, but nothing maps that to a user-facing message.
fix: On runtime failure in generateStrategicDecision (and/or in geminiClient.generateContent), categorize the error (rate-limit/quota vs auth vs network) and surface it: call a new aiConfigStore action to set connectionError (e.g. 'Gemini rate limit reached — AI paused, retrying in 30s') so GeminiSettingsModal/AICommandCenter can show it. Behavior change — flag for owner.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: No AbortController on in-flight Gemini strategic call — late response can resolve after config/key change
detail: generateStrategicDecision (aiEngine.ts:3891) awaits geminiClient.generateContent(prompt) with no AbortController/cancellation. If the user clears their key or disconnects (clearGeminiConfig at aiConfigStore.ts:527 calls geminiClient.disconnect()) while a request is in flight, the awaited promise still resolves, recordApiUsage runs (:3900) charging the cost overlay, and setStrategicPriorities/recordDecision fire for a now-disconnected client. The legacy SDK's generateContent does accept an AbortSignal via requestOptions, so this is wireable. Low because it is a transient correctness/cost-accounting issue, not a crash.
fix: Thread an AbortController through geminiClient.generateContent (pass { signal } as the SDK requestOptions second arg) and abort it from disconnect(). At minimum, re-check geminiClient.isConnected() after the await before recording cost/priorities.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: No global unhandledrejection / window.onerror handler — async rejections outside React are invisible
detail: Grep across src for 'unhandledrejection' / 'window.onerror' / 'onunhandledrejection' returns zero matches. React's ErrorBoundary (main.tsx:53, App.tsx:651) only catches render-phase errors; it does NOT catch unhandled promise rejections (e.g. the Rapier prewarm at main.tsx:10, dynamic imports, audio resume, fetch in modelLoader) or async errors thrown outside the React tree. At launch these fail completely silently with no logging or telemetry, making field diagnosis impossible. main.tsx already monkeypatches console.warn (:17), so it is the natural place to add a listener.
fix: In main.tsx add window.addEventListener('unhandledrejection', e => logger.error('[unhandledrejection]', e.reason)) and a window.addEventListener('error', ...) so background async failures are at least logged. No UX change.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: ConnectionState 'reconnecting' is defined but never set — dead UI state / misleading reconnect contract
detail: types.ts:20 declares `type ConnectionState = 'disconnected' | 'connecting' | 'connected' | 'reconnecting'`. SignalingService.ts:93-97 logs 'attempting reconnect' and relies on PeerJS auto-reconnect, but nothing in MultiplayerManager or the multiplayer store ever calls setConnectionState('reconnecting') (grep: no setter usage of the literal). On PeerJS 'disconnected', the guest path goes straight to handleHostDisconnect (MultiplayerManager.ts:271) / setConnectionState('disconnected') (:103) with no reconnecting phase. Any UI keyed on 'reconnecting' is dead code, and the auto-reconnect is invisible to the user.
fix: Either wire setConnectionState('reconnecting') into the SignalingService 'disconnected' handler (and back to 'connected' on reopen) so the UI reflects the auto-reconnect, or remove the unused literal from the union. First option changes UX — flag for owner if chosen.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: Emoji in user-facing AI decision text violates project No-Emoji rule
detail: CLAUDE.md mandates 'Never use emoji characters in the codebase' (only the 🏭 branding exception). aiEngine.ts:3921 sets `action: \`🧠 Strategic: ${strategic.priorities[0]}\``, and :3913-3914 build reasoning text with 💡 and ⚖️. These strings flow into AIDecision.action/reasoning and are rendered verbatim in the AI decision panels (AICommandCenter). Line 3756 even has to strip the '🧠 Strategic: ' prefix back out, confirming it is real rendered content. Lane-marginal and cosmetic, but a genuine guideline violation in shipped UI text.
fix: Replace the emoji prefixes with Lucide icons in the rendering component (per CLAUDE.md), or drop the inline emoji and keep the label plain ('Strategic: ...', 'Insight: ...', 'Trade-off: ...'). Changes displayed text — flag for owner.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async

title: Context-overflow detection relies on brittle English substring matching of error messages
detail: isContextOverflowError (geminiClient.ts:256-261) matches error.message.toLowerCase() against a hardcoded English phrase list (CONTEXT_OVERFLOW_PATTERNS at :30-37: 'context length','token limit','too long', etc.). The legacy SDK's actual overflow errors carry structured status codes (e.g. INVALID_ARGUMENT) and provider-specific wording that may not contain any of these phrases. When it misses, the overflow is counted as a normal failure (recordFailure at :317), needlessly advancing the circuit breaker toward open instead of triggering the intended heuristic fallback (:310-314). Low because truncation at :293 already caps prompt size, making real overflows rare.
fix: Prefer matching on the SDK error's status/code (e.g. err.status === 'INVALID_ARGUMENT' or HTTP 400 with a token-limit reason) and keep the substring list only as a secondary heuristic.
_area: L4 API/Errors · Gemini + multiplayer proto + error boundaries + async
